text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Priya Menon
Southside Family Practice

12 Greenbank Road, London SE5 8AB
Dear Dr Priya Menon
Amelia Carter, DOB: 14-03-1993, Hospital No: H927451 NHS No. 8475201936
Flat 3, 22 Larkspur Court, London SE17 2JS 

Diagnoses/summary: Generalised epilepsy, postpartum within the last six months; sleep fragmentation with infant care; previous EEG consistent with generalised spike–wave discharges

Neuroimaging: MRI brain (2022) reported normal

Previous and current medication: Valproate (good initial response, see below), Levetiracetam (mood irritability), Lamotrigine (sub-therapeutic response at 100 mg bd, discontinued during pregnancy), Clobazam PRN for clusters; current contraception not established (postpartum, breastfeeding discussed)

Allergies: No known drug allergies

Obstetric/postpartum: Uncomplicated term delivery six months ago; breastfeeding; reports fragmented night-time sleep due to infant care

I reviewed Amelia Carter in clinic today. She attended with her partner and infant. She described that her night-time sleep is broken, typically waking two to three times nightly for feeds, and she feels more fatigued during the day. She is otherwise well with no intercurrent illness.

Seizure history and current frequency: She had no seizures for nearly a year following initiation of Valproate, then developed myoclonic jerks leading to two tonic seizure three Saturdays ago. She reports the myoclonic jerks occurred predominantly in the early morning after a short night, and she attributes the relapse to sleep disruption and stress. There were no reported focal features, tongue injury was minor, and recovery was complete within the day. No injuries to the infant.

Examination: Neurological examination today is normal. Vitals stable. No stigmata of systemic illness.

Discussion: The clinical picture, prior EEG, and seizure semiology remain in keeping with generalised epilepsy. We discussed the likely contribution of sleep fragmentation postpartum to seizure threshold. We reviewed safety measures with infant care, including changing nappies on the floor and avoiding bathing the baby alone. We also discussed medication options compatible with the postpartum period and breastfeeding, alongside contraception counselling and the potential teratogenicity considerations for future pregnancies.

Plan:
- Continue Valproate at current dose pending serum level and LFTs; we will adjust if sub-therapeutic or if further events occur. Bloods today: Valproate trough, FBC, LFTs, U&E.
- Provide Clobazam 10 mg PRN for short-term use during clusters of morning myoclonus (maximum 10 mg bd for up to 3 days); sedation risk discussed.
- Sleep hygiene and support: advised partner-assisted night feeds where possible; consider expressed milk to reduce sleep disruption; avoid sleep deprivation triggers.
- Safety: reinforced seizure precautions with infant care and avoidance of swimming/bathing the baby alone; advised not to co-sleep when drowsy or after Clobazam use.
- Contraception and future pregnancy counselling: signposted to preconception counselling if planning future pregnancy; folic acid 5 mg daily to be considered preconception.
- DVLA advice: instructed that any further generalised tonic-clonic seizures should be reported; she currently does not drive.
- Follow-up: Nurse-led telephone review in 4–6 weeks to review symptoms, side effects, and blood results; clinic review in 3 months, earlier if further seizures occur.

Thank you for your ongoing care and support. Please contact me if there are any concerns or if further information is needed.

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter states: “She had no seizures for nearly a year following initiation of Valproate, then developed myoclonic jerks leading to two tonic seizure three Saturdays ago.” This provides a timeframe of approximately one year with a total of two tonic seizures, implying a frequency of 2 per year. Myoclonic jerks are mentioned but without a numeric frequency, so they cannot be used to set a higher rate. She is not seizure-free for ≥6 months due to a recent event, so a seizure-free label is not applicable.
